item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I now fully understand your points, and I will keep in mind, particularly the fact that I need to improve the tone of my writing style. I apologize if I sounded rude a little, but at several points, I was just confused by some of what you have said.

Hopefully, I rectify in future articles the writing issues you pointed out, because now that I think about it, professional tone is a problem for me. I will also try to make the anomalous part clearer :)

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
I didn’t say it was a joke, I said the pacing wasn’t right to me. I was surprised the ending was “just like that”, if that makes sense.

In my opinion, in the nicest way possible, I can see you on the other side of the screen, writing this. Not a foundation researcher. The writer seems passionate and excited about this topic. It can have a happy ending, but maybe have them report things as

“The case [of the disappearance of the many koalas] was originally thought to be a massive poaching operation that would damage the ecosystem was dismissed, but when the events’ anonymous properties became apparent research into the disappearances quickly began.”

For me it needs to have that sense of, ‘a sad thing is happening and we don’t give squat about it until we can prove it fits under our strict criteria.’ That’s was missing for me in this piece. Some sad moments could make the happy ending have a larger impact.

And about being skeptical, you can go ahead and dismiss my advice as wrong but it is advice and I think you should take it and craft this SCP to be 1% better. People generally don’t like my SCPs and I feel kinda hated on the forum but I’m trying to help people and pitch ideas that haven’t been thought of yet. I’m gonna leave it here before it turns into a rant.

Good luck with your article.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
Offering my two cents as the author, I believe you unfortunately misread the whole article.

So, the first point. It's not anomalous, you say. For context, this article takes place in 2022, two years after the bushfires have occurred. Safe to say, if thousands of koalas1 still disappear at that point, without any fires, that would be utterly anomalous, right? I mean, it's the symbol of Australia, and someone's bound to notice the fact that they're disappearing.

Plus, I don't think large, flying serpents are a species native to Australia, because if that's the case, then I would be saving up in order to ride on those. Second, koalas are cute, and I won't trade those for snakes.

Second, I'm not sure on your thoughts about the pacing. You say "greatly written" at the start but then dismiss my article's pacing and narrative as a joke.

Choose one - it's ok if you found the article unsatisfying, but I'm just really confused about what you said.

Additionally, all people I asked did not point out that my article was too sparse and unnaturally progressing, and I'm against the idea that environmental action against climate change is a joke, so I have my doubts on everything you said.

Lastly, the ending (Incident DROPBEAR) was crafted in order to suit the New Year. Look, I don't want to shoehorn drama, politics2 and angst into this whole thing just to reduce the possible "that's it?"moment, because that would detract from the whole koala and aboriginal culture angle.

Plus, I'm simply basing off the fact that Dreamtime is rooted in the concept of an everywhen - the fact that it is timeless, so I actually don't feel good about you saying that this is a joke.

Footnotes
1. You should also note that even those in zoos - safe from bushfires - disappeared.
2. Australian politics are a whole another level, and as a Filipino, I didn't dare touch on that deeply.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: The Foundation didn’t care about the koalas disappearing due to them wanting to keep nature safe but due to it potentially being marked as anomalous activity and might leading to breaking the secrecy. Hell, in the Broken Masquarade all it took were a couple of relatively small events that led to the total collapse of the Veil, so with such a giant event as an entire species literally disappearing from the planet I believe the Foundation had every reason to intervene to stop a potential break of secrecy.

Greatly written but for me lacks the foundation’s essence. It feels like there’s too much of a person on the other side, rather that a bland office worker who has seen evil things and is complicit.

In my opinion, they shouldn’t care about the decline of the koalas. It’s just an observation. Not every article has to lead you to believe the foundation is f*cked up, even the fact that they look into the (idk what’s the word, environmental, endangered species) is a great new perspective on the foundation. But when I read this I feel like I see you passionate about this topic (and that’s great!), but a Foundation worker will most likely not be.

On a different note, the whole article progression/ending was unsatisfying to me. I’m not a hater of happy endings, but it felt like a bit of a joke moment to me. (“That’s it?” Kind of moment)

(Humbly, in my opinion, who may more may not be correct)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I researched a lot of Dreamtime beliefs and cultural practices for this, so I'm glad you enjoyed this article :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, I love aboriginal mythology and stuff like it, and it helps even more that this one had a happy ending. Easy +1!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: one person's trash is another person's motive to make a sort of treasure, I guess? :P
i genuinely cannot believe people actually want to write for this garbage GOI of mine

item.title: This Author Post is Sponsored by the Koalalition
item.content:encoded:
Hello! Veralta's here with a new SCP about koalas, nature, the future of Australia, and intricate worldbuilding based on the beliefs of aboriginal Australian people like the Bundjalung and the Eora. Consider this SCP a free New Year trip through Australia, and a little bit of a crash course on the Australian condition.

Also, it's linked to Ralliston's Obearwatch Command; fun fact, all of these started when he threw the word "Koalalition" at me, and I said, "Hmm… uwah, this looks interesting". I initially planned out a short article with a gimmick and a punchline.

But then, I realized I had to dive deep into the waterhole.

The aboriginal Australian belief system, also called Dreamtime1, is very complex and interesting. It would be a shame if I just went with a silly punchline when aboriginal culture, with its mix of hopes and dreams for Australia, needs a chance to shine here.

Lastly, consider this article as a piece dedicated to Australians and wildlife - such as the koalas themselves - who have been affected by the severe bushfires. As a Filipino youth, I couldn't do much to be honest, aside from raising awareness. I tried to stay true to the myths' essence while adding my style of writing to them, while I researched news about the bushfires.

All in all, though, please enjoy this SCP, and I wish a Happy New Year - and more happy years - to come for all of us!

Special thanks to Ralliston2, Meisteroni, DrChandra, Rose the Changeling , for concept checking and making my narrative is solid. Dysadron and caspian2 also have my gratitude for their comprehensively good LBL and critique. Last but not the least, I also want to give a well-deserved shoutout to Dr Balthazaar for providing guidance on Australian beliefs and culture. You guys are amazing!

Footnotes
1. By the way, this word does not cover what the beliefs really mean, and while dreaming is a part of the culture, it is not just that. Additionally, the word "Dreamtime" may even be just a mistranslation of words meant for deities.
2. Bear goes rawr :p
